State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Telephone Administration
916 322 5660

Executive Legal
337 5691

Enforcement
337 6641

Conflict of Interest
322 6641

July 19, 1977

77-24-053

Tom Hamilton
Supervisor, First District
County of San Diego
Board of Supervisors
1600 Pacific Highway
San Diego, California 92101

Dear Mr. Hamilton:

This is in response to your letter of 30 June 1977, requesting informal advice on some additional questions tangential to those raised in Opinion Request #77-006 and dealing generally with plans for the proposed South County Regional Center.

Having reviewed your letter of 30 June and having discussed the entire situation at the staff level, it is not possible to discuss the concept of "materiality" because we are not informed of the size and nature of the "source of income" to whom you sold the property. See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702. However, we do not think that if that information is necessary to resolve this matter for the following reasons.

As articulated in the previous exchange of letters between yourself and the Commission, the basic reason for finding that the decision to locate the County Center would "foreseeably have a material financial effect" on the value of the property (and perhaps on the purchaser of said property), was the assumption that the location of the Center would have a generally uplifting effect on the neighborhood in which it was located and would inspire increased commercial and residential activity in said neighborhood. The advice of the office of the County Counsel, which we generally endorsed in denying your request for an opinion, further distinguished between the effect of decisions relating to such matters as location and more "cosmetic" decisions concerning specific details of the project.

Mr. Tom Hamilton
July 19, 1977                                                Page 2


     It is our conclusion that the decisions referred to in your
letter of June 30 fit into the latter category. Accordingly, it
is not "reasonably foreseeable that (those decisions) will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally on....(that) source of income". Government
Code §87103.

     I trust this will resolve your problem. Thank you for
writing. If I can be of further assistance in this or any
matter, please feel free to call on me at (916)322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Kenneth W. Goshorn
                                        Research Specialist III
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

KWG:mfa

COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO

BOARD OF SUPERVISORS • 1600 PACIFIC HIGHWAY
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101 • (714) 236-2249

TCM HAMILTON
SUPERVISOR
FIRST DISTRICT

June 30, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
100 Kay Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Honorable Commission:

On April 22, 1977, I received from the Executive Director
of the Fair Political Practices Commission informal advice
relating to the nature of the "source of income" provisions
as they relate to conflict of interest issues under the
Political Reform Act of 1974 (Opinion Request No. 77-006).
For your information, a copy of both my request and the
informal advice is attached hereto. Matters concerning the
establishment of the South County Regional Center continue to
be of significant importance for the people of my supervisorial
district, and I am now seeking to determine whether under the
Political Reform Act I may participate in that aspect of the
South County Regional Center matter which involves whether the
Board of Supervisors will provide for locating a branch of the
San Diego County Superior Court in that center. I am aware
that the basic test under the Act is whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the decision of the Board of Supervisors to
locate or not to locate a branch of the Superior Court in the
center will have "a material financial effect" on the "source"
of income which I received from the sale of properties, which,
as described in my letter of March 24, 1977, are located
immediately east of the proposed South County Regional Center.

Assuming that the Board of Supervisors has previously made the
determination that a regional center will be established at
this location, I seek a determination of whether the partic-
ular action of the Board of Supervisors with regard to location
of branch Superior Courts would "foreseeably" have an impact
on my source of income.

The proposed South County Regional Center is to be situated
on a 15-acre site, and if the Superior Courts are included,
would contain seven buildings for the following uses:

Fair Political Practices Commission        Page 2        June 30, 1977

(1) Courts and court-related, clerks, and detention
(2) District Attorney and building support
(3) Welfare and human services
(4) Public health and administration
(5) Mental health
(6) Substance Abuse
(7) Probation and general government

The governmental functions to be carried out within the regional
center are the following:

Assessor                                    Auditor and Controller
Building Services                           Communications
County Clerk                                District Attorney
Electronic Data Processing                  Facilities Maintenance
Fleet Operations                            Food Services
Health Care                                 Human Services
Law Library                                 Marshal
Municipal Court (South Bay                  Municipal Court Clerk
   Judicial District)                       Probation Department
Revenue and Recovery                        Sheriff
Superior Court                              Superior Court Administrator
County Supervisor Branch                    Welfare
   Office

It is presently proposed that the Municipal Court facilities would
be sufficient to accommodate seven judges by 1985 and that the
Superior Court facilities would accommodate six judges by that date.

In the event that the branch Superior Court facilities are not
located in the South County Regional Center, one proposed building
would not be constructed as a part of the project, and the District
Attorney and Building Support Facilities would be included in the
space now set aside for the Superior Court.  However, the space
would be available to construct the other building at some future
time in the event the Superior Courts courts were ultimately in-
cluded on the whole need expanded to warrant additional services
being provided from the regional center. County staff currently
estimates that construction of the center with the Superior Courts
would cost approximately $27 million and that construction without
the Superior Courts would cost approximately $25 million. It is
also anticipated that if the Superior Courts are not included
within the center that the staff of the District Attorney and
Probation Department would be reduced by approximately 20%.

The properties which I recently sold are located in the City of
Chula Vista "Town Centre Project Area" and lie within either the
"CO" (Administrative and Professional Office) or the "R-3" (Apart-
ment Residential) Zones.  They presently are used as real estate
offices (two lots) and single-family residences.

Fair Political Practices Commission        Page 3        June 30, 1977


In view of the above, and particularly the fact that the
Municipal Court facilities will be located in the regional center
in any event, it is my request that you advise whether, without
the necessity of addressing the question of the "materiality"
of the potential effect on the "source of income," it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the decision of the Board of Supervisors re-
garding location of the Superior Courts would have an impact on
my source of income, i.e., the purchasers of the properties.

Your efforts to provide me with your opinion as soon as possible
will be appreciated. Mr. Goshorn of your staff was involved in
the review of my previous request.

Sincerely,

Tom Hamilton
Tom Hamilton
Supervisor, First District

TH:hr

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 22, 1977

Honorable Tom Hamilton
Supervisor, First District
County of San Diego
1600 Pacific Highway
San Diego, CA 92101

RE: Opinion Request
No. 77-006

Dear Mr. Hamilton:

Thank you for your letter of March 24, 1977, requesting an opinion concerning a potential conflict of interest you may have in your capacity as a member of the San Diego County Board of Supervisors. Because your opinion request does not raise a substantial question of interpretation under the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will be issued by the Commission. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18320(q)(2). However, I hope the following informal advice will enable you to resolve this matter.

Your basic question is whether the purchaser of the real property you presently own will become a source of income to you within the meaning of Government Code Section 87103(c) or whether the real property itself is the source of income. The opinion which you received from the County Counsel accurately reflects the view of the Fair Political Practices Commission with respect to this question when it concludes that the purchaser of the real property would become a source of income to you. See, e.g., Opinion requested by Iris Sankey, 2 FPPC Opinions ____ (No. 76-071, November 3, 1976). Accordingly, the sale of the real property will not completely free you from the potential conflict of interest about which you are concerned. During the twelve month period following consummation of the sale, it will be necessary, as the County Counsel indicates, to analyze whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision of the Board of Supervisors will have a material financial affect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the purchaser. To the extent that any such decision will have the requisite

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION 916 322 5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322 5641 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322 0441

Honorable Tom Hamilton
April 22, 1977
Page Two


material financial effect, you will be prohibited from making,
participating in making or in any way using your official
position to influence that decision. Government Code Section
87100.

     We also are in general agreement with the advice
of County Counsel that reference should be made to the Com-
mission's regulation clarifying "material financial effect"
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702). However, in light of the
unique factual situation surrounding the sale of your real
property interest, we think that a few additional observa-
tions with respect to the application of that regulation are
pertinent.

     The basic test established by the regulation is
that the financial effect of a governmental decision is
material if "in light of all the circumstances and facts
known at the time of the decision, the official knows or has
reason to know that the existence of the financial interest
might interfere with the performance of his or her duties
in an impartial manner free from bias." 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702(a). In applying this test to your situation,
we think it is significant that the proposed Regional Center
probably will affect the price you will be able to obtain
for the sale of your real property interest. Both you and a
potential purchaser will be aware of the proposed project
and, to the extent the purchaser believes the project will
have a positive effect on the value of your real property,
you will be able to secure a higher price for the sale of
your interest. In other words, it is likely that the purchaser
of the real property will be willing to pay a premium because
the Regional Center is going to enhance the property's value.
It is also conceivable that the premium the purchaser will
be willing to pay might be even higher if he thought that as
a member of the Board of Supervisors you would be able to
participate in decisions concerning the Regional Center.
Finally, it is not unreasonable to assume that the greater
the premium the purchaser is willing to pay, the greater the
likelihood that your impartiality vis-a-vis the purchaser
could be affected.

     We do not mean to suggest by the foregoing observations
that realizing a premium on the sale of your real property
purchase is in any way improper. Furthermore, we do not think that
the analysis means that in every case involving a decision
about the Regional Center the "materiality" requisite to a
conflict of interest necessarily will be present. However,

Honorable Tom Melilton
April 22, 1977
Page Three

we do think the analysis is relevant to an application of
the test established by 2 Cal. Welf. Code Section 18702(a)
and that it should be considered, along with other relevant
factors, in resolving whether you have a conflict of interest
within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100 with
respect to governmental decisions concerning the proposed
Regional Center.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18321. If you have any further questions, please contact
Kenneth Goshorn, an attorney in the Commission's Conflict of
Interest Division at (916) 322-6444.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:KP:plh

Request No. 11 006
Date Received 3-25-977
Response Due 7/8/77

COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO

BOARD OF SUPERVISORS • 1600 PACIFIC HIGHWAY
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101 • (714) 236-2249

TOM HAMILTON
SUPERVISOR
FIRST DISTRICT


March 24, 1977


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Honorable Commission:

I am writing to request the opinion of the Fair Political
Practices Commission with regard to a question involving the
conflict of interest prohibition under the Political Reform
Act of 1974. I am presently the owner of certain real property
located on the southeast corner of Third Avenue and H Street in
the City of Chula Vista, California, directly east of a proposed
county regional center in San Diego County. There is no
question that my interest in this property is a financial
interest within the terms of the Political Reform Act of 1974
as an "interest in real property." I have been advised by the
County Counsel of San Diego County that because of the location
of the parcels and the project proposed by the Board of Super-
visors, any action of the Board to acquire property for the
center, either by purchase or by eminent domain or any action
to change the nature of the regional center, such as through
elimination or addition of any governmental functions therein
would in all probability have a "material economic effect" on
my "interest in real property." I, therefore, have been advised
that my participation in any such actions by the Board would
violate conflict of interest prohibition of the Political
Reform Act of 1974 (Government Code Section 87100). This opinion
of the County Counsel dated February 15, 1977 is attached for
your information.

I am in the process of selling the properties involved and am
currently in escrow which will close sometime between April 1
and April 15, 1977. When this escrow is closed, I will have
divested myself of all interest in said properties and will be
completely "cashed out." It has been my hope that this action

FPPC                          -2-                    March 24, 1977

in selling these properties for cash will allow me to parti-
cipate and vote on the issues before the Board of Supervisors
involving the South County Regional Center and thus truly
represent my constituents. However, the County Counsel in
his opinion of February 15, 1977 has advised that if I were
to dispose of my "interest in real property" and the purchasers
of the property were a "source of income" to me in excess of
$250, such would also constitute an interest cognizable
under the Political Reform Act. The County Counsel further
has concluded that it would be necessary to analyze whether
actions proposed to be taken by the Board of Supervisors
relating to the South County Regional Center and within
twelve months of the date I received the income would have a
"material economic effect" upon the "source of income,"
i.e., the purchasers of the property.

Following a discussion of this conflict of interest problem
between representatives of my office, the San Diego County
District Attorney's Office, and the County Counsel's Office,
the District Attorney provided the County Counsel with an
opinion dated March 7, 1977 (also attached) in which the
District Attorney concluded that the "source of income" in
the above situation would be the real property itself rather
than the purchaser of the property. Based upon this inter-
pretation of the law, the District Attorney also concluded
that I would not risk a violation of the conflict of interest
prohibition if, prior to the time I participated in any
decisions of the regional center as a member of the Board, I
sold my property for cash and closed escrow, without engaging
in a contingent agreement regarding future benefits to the
property by virtue of Board of Supervisors decisions involving
the regional center.

In view of this basic conflict between the County Counsel
and the District Attorney in interpretation of the terms of
the Political Reform Act as to what constitutes a "source of
income," the County Counsel has again advised me by corres-
pondence of March 15, 1977 that it would be appropriate for
me to exercise my rights under Section 83114 of the Government
Code and Section 18329 of Title 2 of the California Administra-
tive Code by requesting that the Fair Political Practices
Commission provide me with an opinion regarding the specific
issue whether the purchaser of the property or the property
itself is the "source of income" in the situation described
above. If the purchasers are determined to be the "source
of income," then I would also appreciate further analysis from

FPPC                    -3-                    March 24, 1977


the Fair Political Practices Commission as to the test for
determining whether an action of the Board of Supervisors would
have a "material economic effect" on the "source of income."
In his opinion of February 15, 1977 the County Counsel concluded
that in addition to analysis of the proposed Board action,
considerations such as the size and other assets and income
of my "source of income" would be relevant to a final con-
clusion. I also understand that an analysis of the definition
of "materiality" pursuant to Section 18702 of Title 2 of the
California Administrative Code would be required.

Currently, matters relating to the South County Regional Center
before the Board of Supervisors have been delayed in order to
receive review and recommendation of the project as it applies
to provision and branching of courts both from the judges of
the affected courts (Government Code Section 68075) and from
the judicial council (Government Code Section 69752). It is
my understanding that the Board may expect some action from
the judicial council in the future. Therefore, I am requesting
that you provide me with an opinion at your earliest possible
convenience in order that I may be in the position to fully
evaluate my options with regard to participation as a member
of the Board of Supervisors on this significant matter.

Thank you very much for your consideration of my request.

Very truly yours,

Tom Hamilton
Tom Hamilton
Supervisor, First District

TH:tu

Enclosures

cc: Each Member of the Board of Supervisors
    District Attorney
    Chief Administrative Officer
    County Counsel

OFFICE OF
THE DISTRICT ATTORNEY
COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO
EDWIN L. MILLER, JR.
DISTRICT ATTORNEY

WILLIAM H. KENNEDY
ASST. DISTRICT ATTORNEY

RICHARD D. HUFFMAN
ASST DISTRICT ATTORNEY

DAVID A. VALLES
ASST DISTRICT ATTORNEY

COUNTY COURTHOUSE
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101
(714) 236-2329
COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO

March 7, 1977

Donald L. Clark
County Counsel
355 County Administration Center
San Diego, California 92101

Dear Don:

On February 28, 1977, a meeting took place in the District
Attorney's Conference Room to discuss conflict of interest
problems relating to Supervisor Hamilton's property in
Chula Vista. As you will recall, those present were your-
self, Bill Schwartz, Peter Lehman, and John Piesinger from
this Office, and two aides attached to Supervisor Hamil-
ton's staff. As a consequence of that meeting, we agreed
to submit our own analysis and opinion regarding what
effect, if any, the sale of certain real property adja-
cent to the proposed South Bay Regional Center by Super-
visor Hamilton would have upon his ability to participate in
all Board decisions upon the Regional Center. Our specific
attention was directed towards whether or not a cash sale
of the property would remove any potential for a conflict
of interest.

As you know, the general conflict of interest prohibition
is stated in Government Code Section 87100, as follows:

"No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his of-
ficial position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest."

"Financial interest" is defined in Government Code Section
87103 as:

"An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect,

Donald L. Clark
Page 2
March 7, 1977

distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the
public official has a direct or indirect
investment worth more than one thousand
dollars ($1,000);

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, other than
loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value received by or promised to the public
official within twelve months prior to the
time when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the
public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management."

As it currently exists, Supervisor Hamilton's real property
interest definitely constitutes a "financial interest" pursuant to Section 87103(b). A cash sale of the property would
convert its characterization to "income," as defined in
Section 82030(a). Additionally, such a sale and conversion
would constitute a "source of income" pursuant to Section
87103(c). The critical question about Section 87103(c) is
whether it is the purchaser, or the property that is the
"source."

Although the Political Reform Act fails to define "source of
income," it is interesting to note that Section 87103 is
nearly identical to the phraseology used in old Government
Code Section 3700, pursuant to the Moscow Act, which was
essentially replaced by the Political Reform Act. "Source
of income" was defined pursuant to the Moscow Act, in old
Government Code Section 3610(e) as:

". . . the business entity or activity of
the official which earned or produced the
income."

Donald L. Clark
Page 3
March 7, 1977


It was the specificity of that definition that kept the
California Supreme Court from determining that disclosures
of sources of income amounted to an unconstitutional in-
vasion of privacy. See, County of Nevada v. MacMillan,
11 Cal.3d 662, 674-675 (1974). Consequently, it is appar-
ent that the new Political Reform Act will have to adopt
a similar definition to withstand a constitutional chal-
lenge. Accordingly, the "source" of Supervisor Hamilton's
income, pursuant to a cash sale of his real property, would
be the property itself, rather than the purchaser. Thus,
the next area of inquiry is whether or not the "source of
income" has any potential for causing a conflict of interest
should Supervisor Hamilton partake in decisions regarding
the South Bay Regional Center.

The Fair Political Practices Commission has noted that four
specific elements must be present before a public official
is required to disqualify himself from participation in a
governmental decision. First, it must be reasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a
financial effect. Second, the anticipated financial
effect must be on a financial interest of the official,
as defined in Sections 87103(a) through (d). Third,
the anticipated financial effect must be material. And
fourth, the governmental decision's anticipated finan-
cial effect on the official's financial interest must
be distinguishable from its effect on the public gen-
erally.

Having already identified the quantum of Supervisor
Hamilton's "financial interest," the only remaining
question is whether or not it is reasonably foreseeable
that any governmental decision regarding the South Bay
Regional Center would have a material financial effect
upon that financial interest. Unquestionably, whatever
effect that is involved would be distinguishable from
the effect upon the public generally.

In 2 California Administrative Code Section 18702, the
Fair Political Practices Commission has elaborated upon
the concept of "materiality" while recognizing that each
case must be evaluated separately. There are three con-
siderations to be examined. First, will the governmental
decision affect the "source of income" by increasing or

Donald L. Clark
Page 4
March 7, 1977


decreasing annualized gross revenue by 1%, or annual net
income by 5%, or by increasing or decreasing assets or
liabilities by $50,000 or more, or by 5% of the current
assets or liabilities, whichever is least? Second, will
the governmental decision affect the amount of income to
be received by the official? And third, is there a
nexus between the governmental decision and the purpose
for which Supervisor Hamilton receives the income?

Since the "source of income" in this instance is the real
property which produced the income, rather than the pur-
chaser, the first and second considerations, noted above,
are inapplicable, so long as the amount of cash involved
is fixed and transferred from escrow to Supervisor Hamil-
ton prior to any decisions regarding the South Bay
Regional Center. Once that is accomplished, the only
remaining question is what connection, if any, exists
between any decision in which Supervisor Hamilton partici-
pated and the purpose for which he received the proceeds?
If the original agreed upon purchase price was dependent
upon a future favorable decision regarding the Regional
Center, that would constitute a "nexus" sufficient to
establish "material financial effect upon the purchaser,
a conflict of interest. However, if no such agreement
had been struck, any subsequent decision on the Regional
Center, though conferring some benefit upon the purchaser,
would not amount to a material financial effect upon
Supervisor Hamilton's "source of income." This is so,
due to the fact that the real property would no longer
be a source of potential financial return. Its benefit
to Supervisor Hamilton would have been realized at the
moment escrow closed, the property was transferred to
the purchaser, and the cash was received by Hamilton.

Consequently, it may be concluded that Supervisor Hamilton
would not risk a conflict of interest, if he sold his
property for cash and closed escrow, without engaging in
a contingent agreement regarding future benefits by virtue
of Board decisions upon the Regional Center, and prior to
the time that he entered into any decisions upon the
Regional Center as a member of the Board.

We are pleased to have been of assistance in this matter.
If there are any questions that we neglected to answer,
don't hesitate to contact us.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
EDWIN L. MILLER, JR.
District Attorney

ELM:bl

County of San Diego
OFFICE OF
COUNTY COUNSEL
355 COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101
(214) 236-3651

DONALD L. CLARK
County Counsel

JOSEPH KASE, JR.
Assistant County Counsel

DEPUTIES
LLOYD H. PARSON, JR.
CATHY E. LEONE
WILLIAM C. GEORGE

MORENO A. HUTCHINS
JOHN H. MORRISON
JANE HANSEN

TIMOTHY R. GARFIELD
JACK POWELL
FRED A. HERBERT
GREGORY M. CARRATT
JON PETERSON
RALPH E. SHAGNETTI
LEONARD W. POLLARD II
ROBERT H. PUDOFF
WILLIAM E. PALMER
JUDITH M. COPELAND
JACK F. BLEVANS
KATHERINE E. STOCKMAN
ROBERTA K. WILKINSON
BRUCE W. BEACH

February 15, 1977


Honorable Tom Hamilton
Supervisor, First District
County of San Diego
335 County Administration Center
San Diego, California 92101

Dear Supervisor Hamilton:

Re: South County Regional Center

You have requested our opinion as to potential conflicts of
interest which you may have regarding actions of the Board
of Supervisors involving the South County Regional Center
at the corner of Third Avenue and "H" Street in the City
of Chula Vista. A map of the site of the proposed Regional
Center accompanied your request, and it shows that you have
an interest in six parcels of property which are for the
most part across Third Avenue from the proposed Regional
Center location.

Your specific request is for an opinion as to what options
you may have to allow you to participate fully on all decisions
regarding the proposed Regional Center. One possible option
which you ask us to discuss would be that of sale of your
interest in the parcels.

Our conclusions may be summarized as follows:

1. Assuming that your interest in any of the six
parcels in question is $1,000 or greater, you have an
"interest in real property" which is recognized under
the Political Reform Act of 1974. Because of the location
of the parcels and the project proposed by the Board of
Supervisors, any action of the Board of Supervisors to
acquire property for the South County Regional Center
either by purchase or by eminent domain or any action to
change the nature of the Regional Center, such as through
elimination or addition of any governmental functions therein
not previously approved by the Board, would in all probability

Supervisor Hamilton                -2-                February 15, 1977

have a "material economic effect" on your "interest in
real property". Therefore, you would have a "financial
interest", and your participation in any such actions would
violate the conflict of interest prohibition of the
Political Reform Act of 1974 (Gov. Code § 87100).

2. In view of the fact that the location and govern-
mental functions of the South County Regional Center were
essentially established by the Board of Supervisors prior
to your assuming the office of County Supervisor, action
by the Board relating merely to consideration and approval
of the schematic or working drawings for the Regional Center
would not have a "material economic effect" on your "interest
in real property" and therefore you would not be prohibited
by the Political Reform Act from participating in such
action.

3. Should the matters regarding consideration of working
drawings become expanded such that basic questions involving
continuation of the project, alteration of the size or function
of the Regional Center, or decentralization of the superior
court, arise before the Board of Supervisors, you would be
prohibited from participating because of your financial
interest.

4. If you were to dispose of your "interest in real
property" and the purchasers of the property were a "source
of income" to you in excess of $250, such would also
constitute an interest cognizable under the Political Reform
Act. It would be necessary to analyze whether actions proposed
to be taken by the Board relating to the South County Regional
Center and within 12 months from the date you received the
income would have a "material economic effect" upon the
"source of income". In addition to analysis of the proposed
Board action, considerations such as the size and other
assets and income of your source of income would be relevant
to a final conclusion.

5. We are aware of no other specific option which you
would have at this time in order to free yourself of potential
conflict of interest with regard to the South County Regional
Center as a result of your "interest in real property".
Deferring income from sale of the properties or placing such
income into a trust would not in our opinion be sufficient to
remove the conflict issue or allow you to participate fully.

DISCUSSION

I
Background Facts

On June 4, 1975, the City Council of Chula Vista adopted
a resolution of public use and necessity for the acquisition

Supervisor Hamilton                    -3-                    February 15, 1977

of an 11.73 acre parcel of property which the city, after
discussions with the County, selected as a site to be used
for the "joint use of the City and County for courthouse and
related facilities". The resolution stated that the City
was undertaking acquisition for the purpose of offering such
property to the County as a site for the facility and the
City would urge the County to consider such property as the
location for the facility and would negotiate with the County
for the use of the property for the facility and to develop
a joint powers agreement for such joint use.

Thereafter, on June 17, 1975 (61) the Board of Supervisors
approved in principle the acquisition of the 3rd and "H" Street
site for a South County Regional Center and authorized negotiations with the City of Chula Vista for the use of the designated
property for the Regional Center. The Board's action was taken
with specific reference to the City's resolution concerning the
above 11.73 acre parcel; however, it is not clear whether the
term "site" is used in the Board action referred to a general
site at 3rd and "H" Streets (boundaries undetermined) or to the
specific 11.73 acre parcel covered by the City resolution.

On December 16, 1975 (15) at the City's request, the
Board of Supervisors scheduled a joint meeting with the City
Council for March 11, 1976. Following the meeting, the Board
authorized the acquisition of that land in the City generally
bordered by 3rd Avenue, "H" Street, "I" Street, and Garrett
Avenue and determined not to adopt a design plan until a
later date, thereby indicating that the project boundaries
remained to be fixed after subsequent reviews (3/11/76 (1)).

Thereafter, on April 6, 1976 (62, 63) the Board accepted
an assignment of the City's option to purchase the 11.73 acre
parcel, subject of the City's June 4, 1975 resolution, and
adopted a resolution of intent to acquire such property.
On April 20, 1976 (71, 72) the Board directed the acquisition of that additional property, if any, required for design
alternative I-B and the inclusion of a Welfare facility and
related parking in the Regional Center thereby determining
the final boundaries of the proposed Regional Center project.

The Board of Supervisors in September 1976 approved
the series and architectural programs for the Regional Center,
and on October 12, 1976 (12) the Board approved a contract
for architectural services. Schematic drawings were prepared
and reviewed with the Board on January 13, 1977, and on
January 25, 1977 (136) the Board approved the schematics,

Supervisor Hamilton                    -4-                    February 15, 1977


but delayed action on the recommendation to direct the
Design Team to proceed with preliminary and working drawings
until February 8, 1977.1/

On February 8, 1977, three items relating to the South
County Regional Center were scheduled for consideration
by the Board of Supervisors. Item 6 was a recommendation
from the Project Management Office that the Board direct
the Design Team to proceed with the preliminary and working draw-
ings and to return to the Board for authorization to call for
bids on work presented at that time. Item 16 was a proposal
by the Director of Real Property relating to purchase of
certain property for the Regional Center. 2/ Item 30
was a proposal also by the Director of Real Property
regarding proposed condemnation of additional property
required for the Regional Center. 3/

During the Board's consideration of Item 6 on February 8,
1977, 4/ County Counsel interpreting the provisions
of the Political Reform Act concluded that from a conflict of
interest standpoint you were not eligible to participate
in any action relating to the condemnation or purchase
of land for the South County Regional Center nor were you
eligible to participate in any Board action which would
change the concept of the Regional Center from that approved
by the Board of Supervisors prior to your assuming your
current position as a County Supervisor. We did advise,


____________________________________________

1/    One reason for the delay was the necessity of receiving
review of and recommendation on the project as it applies to
provision and branching of courts both from the judges of the
affected courts (Gov. Code § 68073) and from the judicial
council (Gov. Code § 69752).

2/    You filed a conflict of interest declaration on this item
on February 1, 1977.

3/    By correspondence of January 10, 1977 pursuant to your
request of January 7, 1977, we advised that based upon your
interest in the property across Third Avenue from the proposed
site you should not participate in Agenda Item 23 appearing
before the Board of Supervisors on January 11, 1977, which was
a proposal for the Board to declare its intention to adopt a
resolution of public necessity to condemn certain other land
for the South County Regional Center and to set a public hear-
ing on the matter.

4/    The Board of Supervisors had originally determined to
consider Item 6 and Item 16 together, but separated them at
your request because of the conflict of interest issue.

Supervisor Hamilton                -5-                February 15, 1977

however, that you were authorized to participate in Board
action on the recommendation that the Design Team be
directed to proceed with preliminary and working drawings,
and, during the discussion of Item 6, we further concluded
that you would be eligible to participate in an action which
would so direct, but would also delay any action regarding the
superior court area until the report from the judicial
council had been received. That action was taken by the
Board (2/8/77 (64)).

Looking to possible future issues, we also opined that
should questions arise whether to establish the Regional Center
of decentralization of the court system as it relates to
the Regional Center, or of alteration of the size or the
concept of the Regional Center arise, you would not be
eligible to participate in view of your financial interest.

II
Analysis of Political Reform Act

Your duties and responsibilities under the general
prohibition sections of the Political Reform Act of 1974 5/
are stated as follows:

"No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest." (Gov. Code § 87100.)

As pertinent to the specific situation presented,
Section 87103 states:

"An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on:

*        *        *

"(b) Any real property in which the
public official has a direct or indirect inter-
est worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

"(c) Any source of income, other than
loans by a commercial lending institution

_________________________________
5/ Title 9 (commencing with § 81000) of the Government Code.
Unless otherwise indicated, all section references hereafter
are to the Government Code.

sections.facts:
Facts

On June 4, 1975, the City Council of Chula Vista adopted a resolution of public use and necessity for the acquisition

of an 11.73 acre parcel of property which the city, after discussions with the County, selected as a site to be used for the "joint use of the City and County for courthouse and related facilities". The resolution stated that the City was undertaking acquisition for the purpose of offering such property to the County as a site for the facility and the City would urge the County to consider such property as the location for the facility and would negotiate with the County for the use of the property for the facility and to develop a joint powers agreement for such joint use.

Thereafter, on June 17, 1975 (61) the Board of Supervisors approved in principle the acquisition of the 3rd and "H" Street site for a South County Regional Center and authorized negotiations with the City of Chula Vista for the use of the designated property for the Regional Center. The Board's action was taken with specific reference to the City's resolution concerning the above 11.73 acre parcel; however, it is not clear whether the term "site" as used in the Board action referred to a general site at 3rd and "H" Streets (boundaries undetermined) or to the specific 11.73 acre parcel covered by the City resolution.

On December 16, 1975 (15) at the City's request, the Board of Supervisors scheduled a joint meeting with the City Council for March 11, 1976. Following the meeting, the Board authorized the acquisition of that land in the City generally bordered by 3rd Avenue, "H" Street, "I" Street, and Garrett Avenue and determined not to adopt a design plan until a later date, thereby indicating that the project boundaries remained to be fixed after subsequent reviews (3/11/76 (1)).

Thereafter, on April 6, 1976 (62, 63) the Board accepted an assignment of the City's option to purchase the 11.73 acre parcel, subject of the City's June 4, 1975 resolution, and adopted a resolution of intent to acquire such property. On April 20, 1976 (71, 72) the Board directed the acquisition of that additional property, if any, required for design alternate I-B and the inclusion of a Welfare facility and related parking in the Regional Center thereby determining the final boundaries of the proposed Regional Center project.

The Board of Supervisors in September 1976 approved the service and architectural programs for the Regional Center, and on October 12, 1976 (12) the Board approved a contract for architectural services. Schematic drawings were prepared and reviewed with the Board on January 13, 1977, and on January 25, 1977 (136) the Board approved the schematics,

but delayed action on the recommendation to direct the Design Team to proceed with preliminary and working drawings until February 8, 1977.

On February 8, 1977, three items relating to the South County Regional Center were scheduled for consideration by the Board of Supervisors. Item 6 was a recommendation from the Project Management Office that the Board direct the Design Team to proceed with the preliminary and working drawings and to return to the Board for authorization to call for bids on work presented at that time. Item 16 was a proposal by the Director of Real Property relating to purchase of certain property for the Regional Center. Item 30 was a proposal also by the Director of Real Property regarding proposed condemnation of additional property required for the Regional Center.

During the Board's consideration of Item 6 on February 8, 1977, County Counsel interpreting the provisions of the Political Reform Act concluded that from a conflict of interest standpoint you were not eligible to participate in any action relating to the condemnation or purchase of land for the South County Regional Center nor were you eligible to participate in any Board action which would change the concept of the Regional Center from that approved by the Board of Supervisors prior to your assuming your current position as a County Supervisor. We did advise,

1/ One reason for the delay was the necessity of receiving review of and recommendation on the project as it applies to provision and branching of courts both from the judges of the affected courts (Gov. Code § 68073) and from the judicial council (Gov. Code § 69752).

2/ You filed a conflict of interest declaration on this item on February 1, 1977.

3/ By correspondence of January 10, 1977 pursuant to your request of January 7, 1977, we advised that based upon your interest in the property across Third Avenue from the proposed site you should not participate in Agenda Item 23 appearing before the Board of Supervisors on January 11, 1977, which was a proposal for the Board to declare its intention to adopt a resolution of public necessity to condemn certain other land for the South County Regional Center and to set a public hearing on the matter.

4/ The Board of Supervisors had originally determined to consider Item 6 and Item 16 together, but separated them at your request because of the conflict of interest issue.

however, that you were authorized to participate in Board action on the recommendation that the Design Team be directed to proceed with preliminary and working drawings, and, during the discussion of Item 6, we further concluded that you would be eligible to participate in an action which would so direct, but would also delay any action regarding the superior court area until the report from the judicial council had been received. That action was taken by the Board (2/8/77 (64)).

Looking to possible future issues, we also opined that should questions of whether to establish the Regional Center, of decentralization of the court system as it relates to the Regional Center, or of alteration of the size or the concept of the Regional Center arise, you would not be eligible to participate in view of your financial interest.

II
Analysis of Political Reform Act

Your duties and responsibilities under the general prohibition sections of the Political Reform Act of 1974 5/ are stated as follows:

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." (Gov. Code § 87100.)

As pertinent to the specific situation presented, Section 87103 states:

"An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

embedding.qa_text: July 19, 1977 Tom Hamilton Supervisor, First District County of San Diego Board of Supervisors 1600 Pacific Highway San Diego, California 92101 Dear Mr. Hamilton: This is in response to your letter of 30 June 1977, requesting informal advice on some additional questions tangential to those raised in Opinion Request #77-006 and dealing generally with plans for the proposed South County Regional Center. Having reviewed your letter of 30 June and having discussed the entire situation at the staff level, it is not possible to discuss the concept of "materiality" because we are not informed of the size and nature of the "source of income" to whom you sold the property. See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702. However, we do not think that that information is necessary to resolve this matter for the following reasons. As articulated in the previous exchange of letters between yourself and the Commission, the basic reason for finding that the decision to locate the County Center would "foreseeably have a material financial effect" on the value of the property (and perhaps on the purchaser of said property), was the assumption that the location of the Center would have a generally uplifting effect on the neighborhood in which it was located and would inspire increased commercial and residential activity in said neighborhood. The advice of the office of the County Counsel, which we generally endorsed in denying your request for an opinion, further distinguished between the effect of decisions relating to such matters as location and more "cosmetic" decisions concerning specific details of the project. Mr. Tom Hamilton July 19, 1977 It is our conclusion that the decisions referred to in your letter of June 30 fit into the latter category. Accordingly, it is not "reasonably foreseeable that (those decisions) will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on... (that) source of income". Government Code §87103. I trust this will resolve your problem. Thank you for writing. If I can be of further assistance in this or any matter, please feel free to call on me at (916) 322-6444. Sincerely, [handwritten signature] Kenneth W. Goshorn Research Specialist III Conflicts of Interest Division KWG:mfa June 30, 1977 Fair Political Practices Commission 100 Kay Street Sacramento, California 95814 Honorable Commission: On April 22, 1977, I received from the Executive Director of the Fair Political Practices Commission informal advice relating to the nature of the "source of income" provisions as they relate to conflict of interest issues under the Political Reform Act of 1974 (Opinion Request No. 77-006). For your information, a copy of both my request and the informal advice is attached hereto. Matters concerning the establishment of the South County Regional Center continue to be of significant importance for the people of my supervisorial district, and I am now seeking to determine whether under the Political Reform Act I may participate in that aspect of the South County Regional Center matter which involves whether the Board of Supervisors will provide for
